originalTitle: Former Nigerian minister sentenced to 75 years in rare corruption verdict
title: A Big Lesson in Fairness from Nigeria!
content:
Hello from Sunny Africa!

Hello, amazing friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, bringing you a very important story from a super big country in Africa called Nigeria. Get ready to learn about rules, fairness, and why being honest is always the best way to be!

Did you know? Nigeria is a very large country in West Africa! It's often called the 'Giant of Africa' because it has so many wonderful people and so much to see!

Who is a 'Minister'?

Imagine your school has a head teacher who helps make sure everything runs smoothly. Well, a country also has people who help run it. These important helpers are called 'ministers'. They have big jobs helping the president and making sure different parts of the country work well.

For example, there might be a minister for schools, who makes sure kids get to learn. Or a minister for health, who helps build hospitals so everyone can stay healthy.

What is 'Power' Anyway?

Our story is about a man who used to be a 'Power Minister' in Nigeria. When we say 'power' here, we don't mean super strength like a superhero! We mean electricity. Electricity is what makes your lights turn on, your TV work, and your mom's phone charge!

The Power Minister's job was to make sure that people in Nigeria had electricity for their homes, schools, and businesses. It's a very important job, because everyone needs electricity!

Did you know? Electricity is super useful! It powers refrigerators to keep food fresh, and helps people cook warm meals. Imagine life without electricity – it would be very dark after sunset!

What is 'Corruption'? (And Why It's Not Good)

Now, here comes a big word: 'corruption'. It might sound tricky, but it just means someone using their special job or power in a wrong way to help themselves, instead of helping everyone else. It's like if you were in charge of sharing cookies, but you took all the biggest ones for yourself and left tiny crumbs for your friends. That wouldn't be fair, right?

When someone in a big job uses money that belongs to the country for themselves, instead of using it to build schools or hospitals, that's called corruption. It's like taking candy from the candy jar when you weren't supposed to, but on a much bigger, more serious scale.

Did you know? Being honest means telling the truth and doing the right thing, even when no one is watching you. It makes people trust you!

What Happened to Mr. Mamman?

Our story is about a former Power Minister named Saleh Mamman. Sadly, some people said he did something wrong when he was in his big job. They said he didn't play fair with the country's money, money that was supposed to help everyone get electricity and make Nigeria a better place.

When someone is accused of doing something wrong, there are special people called judges who listen very carefully to all the stories and look at all the clues. They are like super detectives who make sure everyone is treated fairly and that the rules are followed.

The Judge's Decision

After looking at everything, a judge made a very important decision. The judge said that Mr. Mamman did indeed do wrong things with the money. This is called being 'convicted'. It means the judge found him guilty.

Because what he did was very serious, the judge gave him a very big punishment. It was like saying, 'What you did was not fair, and it caused big problems for many people.' It's like when you break a rule at school, and the teacher gives you a timeout or extra chores. This punishment was much, much bigger because his actions were much, much bigger.

Did you know? Judges wear special robes in a special room called a courtroom. Their job is super important because they help make sure justice is served and rules are followed by everyone.

Where is Mr. Mamman Now?

Now, here's a tricky part of the story. After the judge said Mr. Mamman did wrong, he was supposed to be there to hear his punishment. But guess what? The people who needed to talk to him couldn't find him! They don't know where he is.

This is a bit like playing hide-and-seek, but it's not a fun game. It's important for everyone to face the results of their actions, especially when they have done something wrong that affects many people. So, people are looking for him so he can understand and accept his punishment.

Why This Story Matters to All of Us

This story from Nigeria might seem like it's about grown-ups, but it teaches us all a very important lesson. It shows us that rules are for everyone, no matter how big or important you are. Even leaders have to follow the rules and be honest.

When people in big jobs are honest and play fair, the whole country becomes a better place. Money can be used to build new schools, make sure everyone has clean water, and build better roads. But when there is corruption, these good things don't happen.

Did you know? When leaders are honest, they can use the country's money to build beautiful parks, new libraries, and even fun places for kids to play! It makes everyone's life better.

Let's All Be Fair and Honest!

So, what can we learn from this? We can learn that being honest and fair is always the best way. Always tell the truth, always play fair with your friends, and always follow the rules.

We hope that everyone in Nigeria and all over Africa will work together to make sure that leaders are honest and use their power to help everyone. It makes our world a brighter, happier, and fairer place for all of us!
summary: A former Power Minister in Nigeria, Saleh Mamman, was found guilty of corruption by a judge, but the authorities do not know where he is to face his big punishment.
whyMatters: This story matters because it teaches us that everyone, even important leaders, must follow the rules and be honest, so that money can be used to help build schools and make our country better for everyone.

originalTitle: 'They shot my neighbour in the head' - the lakeside city traumatised by war
title: Our Friends in Uvira Need Peace
content:
Hello from Our African Family!

Hello, my super friends! It's your kid reporter from Africa here, ready to share a story with you. Today, we are going to talk about a place called Uvira. Can you say U-vi-ra? It's a city in a big country named DR Congo. DR Congo is also known as the Democratic Republic of Congo. It's a beautiful country, full of amazing people, just like you and me! Uvira is a very special city because it sits right next to a big, big lake. This lake is called Lake Tanganyika. Imagine a lake so big it looks like an ocean! That's Lake Tanganyika. It has so many fish and beautiful birds all around it. People in Uvira love their lake. They fish, they play by the water, and they watch the beautiful sunsets. It's a place where children usually laugh and play all day long. They go to school, they kick a ball, and they help their mummies and daddies.

What Happened in Uvira?

But sometimes, sad things happen even in beautiful places. Recently, our friends in Uvira had a very, very scary time. There were some groups of people who were fighting. Imagine if your friends started fighting in the playground, but much, much bigger and scarier. When people fight like this, it is called conflict. And it makes everyone feel very, very sad and worried. In December, some people came into Uvira city and there was fighting. It was not a happy time at all. People heard loud noises and they felt very scared. They had to hide to be safe. It was a time when children could not go out to play. They could not go to school to learn. Many people felt very, very afraid for their families and their neighbours. This kind of fighting is called war, and war is never a good thing. It makes everyone sad and hurt. People say that some bad things happened during this fighting. People were hurt, and homes were broken. **Did you know?** The Democratic Republic of Congo is the second-largest country in Africa! It is so big, it can fit many, many countries inside it.

How It Felt for the Children

Imagine waking up and hearing scary sounds instead of birds singing. Imagine not being able to go to school to see your friends and learn new things. This is how many children in Uvira felt. They missed their friends. They missed their teachers. They missed playing their favourite games outside. When something very sad or scary happens, like the fighting in Uvira, it can make people feel very sad, very scared, and sometimes even angry. They might have bad dreams. This feeling of being very sad and scared for a long time is sometimes called being “traumatised.” It means their hearts and minds felt very heavy and worried because of what they saw or heard. Children need to feel safe to learn and grow. When there is fighting, safety goes away. They just want to feel safe again. They want to play hide-and-seek without really hiding from danger. They want to kick their football without worrying about loud sounds. They want to go back to their regular lives, full of sunshine and laughter. **Did you know?** Lake Tanganyika is the second-oldest freshwater lake in the world! It's also the second-deepest, after Lake Baikal in Russia. It's truly a natural wonder!

The Beautiful City of Uvira

Let's remember how wonderful Uvira is, usually. Before the scary time, Uvira was a busy city. People would go to the market to buy fresh food, fruits, and vegetables. They would sell fish caught from the big lake. You could hear music playing and people chatting. Children would run around, playing tag or chasing each other. There are schools where kids learn to read and write and count. There are churches and mosques where people go to pray and feel close to God. There are trees and flowers everywhere. The air smells fresh, especially near the lake. The sun shines brightly almost every day. It's a place where families live together, share meals, and tell stories. The fighting made all these wonderful things stop for a while. Homes were damaged, and some people had to leave their homes to find a safer place. They became what we call “displaced,” meaning they had to move away from their own homes. **Did you know?** The DR Congo is home to amazing animals like mountain gorillas, okapis (a striped giraffe-like animal), and forest elephants! These animals need peace too, to live safely in their homes.

Helping Each Other and Building Back

Even after scary times, people are strong. People in Uvira are brave and kind. When the fighting stopped, people started to help each other. That's what we do in Africa, right? We are one big family. Neighbours helped neighbours. Friends shared what little food they had. People started to clean up and fix their homes. Doctors and nurses helped people who were hurt. Teachers wanted to open their schools again so children could come back and learn. It takes a lot of time and effort to make everything good again after a scary time. But people in Uvira are working very hard. They want their city to be peaceful and happy once more. They want their children to laugh and play without any worries. They want the market to be full of happy shoppers again. They want the sound of music, not scary noises. This helping and working together is very, very important. It shows how much people care about each other. **Did you know?** The capital city of DR Congo is Kinshasa. It's a very big and lively city, far away from Uvira, but still part of the same beautiful country!

Why Peace is So Important

This story from Uvira reminds us of something very, very important: peace. Peace means no fighting. Peace means everyone is safe. Peace means we can all play and learn and live happily together. When there is peace, children can go to school every day. When there is peace, families can be together and feel safe in their homes. When there is peace, cities like Uvira can grow and be beautiful and full of happy sounds. We all want peace, for Uvira and for every place in Africa, and all over the world. We hope that the people who were fighting will stop forever and learn to talk instead. Talking helps us solve problems much better than fighting. Our friends in Uvira deserve to live in peace. Let's send them our good thoughts and hopes for a very peaceful and happy future. Always remember, sharing and caring and peace are the best ways to live. Let’s all be friends, just like we are.
summary: Children and families in Uvira, a city in DR Congo, had a scary time when there was fighting, but now people are helping each other to bring peace and happiness back.
whyMatters: It's important to know about Uvira so we can understand why peace is so special and why everyone deserves to feel safe and happy in their homes, especially children.

originalTitle: African fans face World Cup issues despite visa bond U-turn
title: African fans face World Cup issues despite visa bond U-turn
content: The Trump administration waives a $15,000 visa bond for fans from five countries who have World Cup tickets, but African fans still face challenges.

originalTitle: Eurovision braces for new protests over Israel’s participation
title: A Big Music Party and Important Feelings!
content:
Hello, Friends! Your Africa Reporter is Here!

Hello, my amazing friends! I'm your super friendly news reporter, bringing you stories from all over our big, wonderful world. Today, we're talking about something very exciting that also has some important feelings around it.

What is Eurovision? A Super Fun Song Show!

Imagine a giant, sparkling party! At this party, many different countries send their very best singers and dancers. They sing beautiful songs, catchy tunes, and sometimes even do cool dances! This super special music party is called the Eurovision Song Contest. It's like a big, big music competition where countries show off their unique music and talent. Millions and millions of people all around the world watch it on their TVs and computers. Countries mostly from Europe, but even some other friends like Australia, join in the fun! Eurovision is a time for sharing cultures, making new friends through music, and having a lot of fun. Everyone cheers for their favourite songs and wishes their country good luck. It's a truly happy time for music lovers. Did you know? The very first Eurovision show happened in 1956! That was a super long time ago, even before your grandparents were born! Every year, a different country gets to be the host, which means they get to throw the big party. This year, the party is happening in a country called Sweden. They have been working very hard to make it a great show!

The Grand Final is Almost Here!

The Eurovision show is getting ready for its most exciting part! This special night is called the "Grand Final." It's like the very last game in a big sports tournament, but for singing! All the best singers who have made it through the earlier rounds get to sing one more time. Everyone waits to see who will win the big, shiny trophy and be crowned the champion of Eurovision. It's a really exciting night filled with music, lights, and lots of cheers. People from all the different countries watch with happy hearts, hoping their song wins!

But Wait, What are "Protests"?

Sometimes, even at fun and happy events like Eurovision, people have very important things they want to talk about. When many people gather together to show how much they care about something, we call it a "protest." They might hold up colourful signs with messages, sing special songs, or walk together in a big group. They do this because they want to tell everyone, "Hey, please listen to us! This is very important!" It's their way of using their voice loudly and clearly to try and make a change, or to simply show how they feel deep in their hearts. Did you know? People protest all over the world for many different reasons! Sometimes, they protest for cleaner air, or for good schools for every child, or for people to be treated kindly. It's a way for people to stand up for what they believe is right.

Why Are People Protesting at Eurovision This Year?

This year, some people are planning to protest outside the Eurovision show in Sweden. They are feeling sad and unhappy about some things that are happening very, very far away from the music party. They are thinking about a country called Israel. In Israel, and in some places nearby, there has been some fighting and sadness. When there is fighting, people get hurt, and many, many people become very sad. Homes can be broken, and children might feel scared. The people who are protesting want to show everyone that they really care about peace. They want to tell the world that they are sad about the fighting and they wish for peace and kindness for everyone in Israel and everywhere else. They believe that because Israel is one of the countries taking part in the Eurovision show, it's a good place to talk about these strong and important feelings. Did you know? Music can sometimes help people feel better even when they are sad, and it can help people understand each other's feelings.

What Are the Show Organisers Doing?

The people who work very hard to make the Eurovision show happen are called the "organisers." Their main job is to make sure that everyone has fun and that everyone is safe. They know that people have many different feelings, including very strong ones. So, the organisers are working extra hard to make sure the music show is still a happy and safe place for all the singers and dancers. They also want to make sure that everyone who comes to watch the show is safe, whether they are inside enjoying the music or outside sharing their important feelings. It can be a little tricky, but they try their very best to keep everything calm and smooth. Did you know? Organisers plan for many, many months, sometimes even a whole year, to make a big show like Eurovision happen! It takes a lot of careful work and many helpers.

Why is Music Important For Us All?

At its heart, Eurovision is all about music, and music is a truly wonderful thing! Music can bring people together, even if they come from very different countries and speak different languages. It helps us share our stories, our feelings, and our dreams without even needing words sometimes. Music makes us want to dance, sing along, and smile. Even when there are sad things happening in the world, music can give us hope and make us feel a little bit better. It reminds us that we are all part of one big, colourful world, and we can all share kindness. Did you know? Singing together with friends can make people feel very happy and connected, like one big family! We all hope that by the end of the day, everyone can remember the joy and power of music, and that we can all wish for peace and happiness everywhere.
summary: Some people are planning to protest at the big Eurovision music show because they are sad about fighting in a country called Israel and want everyone to wish for peace.
whyMatters: It's important to remember that even at fun events, some people feel sad about faraway problems and want to speak up for peace, showing us that people care deeply about kindness and a peaceful world for everyone.
